title-group.article-title: Long‐COVID in children and their parents: A prospective cohort study
title-group.alt-title: Long‐COVID in children and their parents

aff.named-content: Department of General Pediatrics and Interdisciplinary Medicine
aff.institution: National Center for Child Health and Development

aff.named-content: Division of Infectious Diseases, Department of Medical Subspecialties
aff.institution: National Center for Child Health and Development

author-notes.corresp:
* Correspondence
Hiroyuki Iijima, Department of General Pediatrics & Interdisciplinary Medicine, National Center for Child Health and Development, 2‐10‐1 Okura, Setagaya‐ku, Tokyo 157‐8535, Japan. Email: iijima-hi@ncchd.go.jp

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Pediatrics International published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd on behalf of Japan Pediatric Society.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Long‐COVID is a significant global health concern, regardless of age. However, few reports have longitudinally evaluated the characteristics, prevalence, and risk factors of long‐COVID in children.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Participants were Japanese children younger than 18 years hospitalized for COVID‐19 between November 2021 and October 2022, along with their COVID‐19 affected parents. During hospitalization and at 1‐, 3‐, and 6‐month follow‐ups, participants completed age‐appropriate questionnaires on long‐COVID symptoms. The quality of life (QOL) score was assessed in children older than 2 years. The prevalence of long‐COVID symptoms by age group was compared. Multivariable logistic regression analysis was conducted to investigate risk factors affecting long‐COVID. Analysis of covariance adjusted for potential confounders was conducted to determine which symptoms affect QOL score.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Of 108 children enrolled, the prevalence of long‐COVID was 44.9%, 37.8%, and 22.8% at 1, 3, and 6 months, respectively, after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. There were no specific risk factors for long‐COVID. Cough, fatigue, and sleep disturbance were the most common long‐COVID symptoms, with sleep disturbance associated with a change in lower QOL score from admission at all three follow‐ups (mean difference 9.25, 20.15, and 19.81; 95% CI, 1.58–16.91, 3.38–36.92, and 5.51–34.11). The prevalence of myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) symptoms among 0–6 years was significantly lower than among 7–17 years and parents; there was no significant difference between 7 and 17 years and parents.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Even 6 months after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection, 22.8% of pediatric patients still had long‐COVID symptoms. Some of these symptoms were similar to those of ME/CFS, potentially affecting children's QOL.

institution-wrap.institution: Japan Society for the Promotion of Science

p.mixed-citation: Iijima H , Funaki T , Kubota M . Long‐COVID in children and their parents: A prospective cohort study. Pediatr Int. 2025;67:e70042. 10.1111/ped.70042 40351239 PMC12067157

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: The pandemic of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19), caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2), has precipitated a global health crisis, with a high risk of infection in both children and adults worldwide. The development of various treatments and vaccines has contributed to the recovery of patients affected by COVID‐19. 1 , 2 , 3 However, recent reports have shown that a number of patients continue to describe ongoing health problems after the acute phase of COVID‐19. These long‐lasting COVID‐19 symptoms and complications, referred to as long‐COVID or post‐COVID‐19 conditions, are major problems worldwide. 4 , 5 , 6

The majority of publications researching these conditions have focused on adult populations. 7 , 8 , 9 , 10 In contrast, the characteristics, prevalence, and risk factors of long‐term sequelae of COVID‐19 in children remain poorly characterized. Given the vast number of children and adolescents worldwide who have suffered from COVID‐19, 11 , 12 there is an urgent need for a better understanding of the persistent symptoms and long‐term complications of COVID‐19. Although there are a few systematic reviews focusing on sequalae of COVID‐19 in children, the results were highly variable because they were mostly cross‐sectional studies with different timeframes and age groups. 13 In addition, the differences in sequelae between children and adults remain unclear, as does the association between sequelae and children's quality of life (QOL). Therefore, prospective studies of appropriate subjects are strongly desired.
We therefore conducted a longitudinal study to investigate the prevalence, risk factors, and impact of long‐term symptoms in children after acute SARS‐CoV‐2 infection.

sec.title: Study design and participants
sec.p: This is a prospective cohort study to examine long‐term symptoms of COVID‐19 among children. We recruited children younger than 18 years hospitalized with confirmed COVID‐19 at the National Center for Child Health and Development (NCCHD) and their parents. The NCCHD is a children's hospital in Tokyo, Japan, serving as the primary COVID‐19 hospital for children in the catchment area. Our hospital has a pediatric intensive care unit and accepts pediatric patients with mild‐to‐severe COVID‐19. The planned recruiting period was from November 2021 to October 2022; because of the excess number of patients under 3 years old, children aged 0–2 years were not recruited from August to October 2022. Patients included in this study were followed for 6 months after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. Eligibility criteria for children were age younger than 18 years and a COVID‐19 diagnosis confirmed by a nucleic acid amplification test or an antigen test for SARS‐CoV‐2. Exclusion criteria were children with special healthcare needs including chromosomal abnormalities or immunodeficiency (either primary or secondary), 14 children who do not live with their parents, and children or parents who do not read or understand Japanese. Parents who were not infected with SARS‐CoV‐2 at the same time as their children were also excluded. This study was approved by the Ethics Committees of the NCCHD (approval no. 2021‐110). Participation in this study was obtained through verbal consent from parents over the phone while their children were hospitalized, followed by written informed consent; assent was obtained from children over 6 years old.

sec.title: Definition of long‐COVID and data collection
sec.p: The definition of long‐lasting COVID‐19 symptoms has not yet been established. In this study, we defined long‐COVID as the persistence of multisystem symptoms or long‐term complications beyond 4 weeks from the date of SARS‐CoV‐2 infection according to previous studies and the NICE definition. 5 , 7 , 13 , 15 The staff pediatricians collected all data during hospitalization and after discharge. The data set during acute hospitalization included demographics, clinical symptoms (Table S1), and severity of COVID‐19. We also classified some symptoms according to the presence of respiratory symptoms (cough and rhinorrhea) and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) symptoms (fatigue, postexertional malaise, sleep disturbance, myalgia, arthralgia, and headache). This definition of ME/CSF symptoms conformed to the essential symptoms of Canadian consensus criteria for ME/CSF. 16 It was unnecessary to strictly meet the diagnostic criteria for ME/CFS because our goal was not to diagnose ME/CFS, but only to collect the primary symptoms of ME/CFS. The severity of acute COVID‐19 was classified according to World Health Organization guidelines. 17 Symptoms of long‐COVID were collected using questionnaires mailed at 1, 3, and 6 months after the SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. If parents were affected with COVID‐19 at the same time as their children, parental symptoms of long‐COVID were also collected. Given that long‐COVID symptoms can emerge after a delay from the acute phase, we investigated symptoms present at each follow‐up rather than those persisting from the acute phase of COVID‐19. The QOL of children was assessed through the KINDL® questionnaire, the measure validated and used internationally. 18 The Kiddy‐KINDL® questionnaire was answered by parents of children aged 3–6 years and KINDL® questionnaire by parents of children aged 7–17 years. The total score calculated ranged from 0 to 100, with higher scores indicating better QOL.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Descriptive results are presented with median and interquartile range (IQR) for continuous data and with numbers and percentages for categorical data. Categorical data were compared using the Fisher's exact test. The p values were adjusted using Bonferroni adjustment methods. We performed multivariable logistic regression analysis to investigate associations of age, sex, severity of acute COVID‐19, and vaccination against SARS‐CoV‐2 with long‐COVID symptoms among children. To investigate the association between long‐COVID symptoms and QOL score of children, analysis of covariance (ANCOVA) was performed, adjusted for their age and sex. Missing values were excluded from each analysis. A p value (two‐sided) less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. All statistical analyses were performed using SPSS, version 27 (IBM, Armonk, NY, USA).

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: In this study, 129 eligible children with confirmed COVID‐19 (including three pairs of siblings) and 126 parents were recruited. Consequently, our cohort comprised 108 children (84% of the recruited children) and 78 parents who were infected with SARS‐CoV‐2 at the same time as their children. Figure 1 shows the number of respondents to the questionnaires. The median age of children was 2.4 (0.6–7.2) years and 49 (45%) were girls. Half of the children had nonsevere disease during the acute phase. Demographic characteristics, treatment, and outcomes are summarized in Table 1. No patient with COVID‐19 had ME/CFS at the time of admission. The main symptoms remaining at discharge were cough (25.0%), rhinorrhea (21.3%), fatigue (4.6%), and diarrhea (2.8%).

caption.p: Enrollment of participants. Recruited participants were children (<18 years old) who were hospitalized with COVID‐19 and their parents. Parents answered the questionnaire only if they were infected with SARS‐CoV‐2 at the same time as their children. *Including three pairs of siblings.

fn.p: Only parents who affected with COVID‐19 at the same time as their children.

sec.p: Table 2 shows the prevalence of symptoms among children at each evaluation point. The prevalence of any symptoms was 44.9%, 37.8%, and 22.8% at 1‐, 3‐, and 6‐month follow‐ups, respectively. In multivariable logistic regression analysis, age, sex, severity of acute COVID‐19, and vaccination against SARS‐CoV‐2 were all not associated with long‐COVID (Table 3). Comparison of clinical characteristics and demographic data for children in the long‐COVID and nonlong‐COVID groups at 6‐month follow‐up showed no significant differences between the two groups (Table S2). Limited to ME/CFS symptoms, multivariate logistic regression analysis (explanatory variables were only age and sex) revealed that increasing age was associated with the development of ME/CFS symptoms (odds ratio 1.17, 1.15, and 1.17; 95% CI, 1.05–1.29, 1.04–1.28, and 1.02–1.33 at 1‐, 3‐, and 6‐month follow‐ups, respectively) (Table S3).

caption.p: Comparison of long‐COVID symptoms among children (0–17 years) and parents.
Multivariable logistic regression to examine risk factors for long‐COVID.

sec.p: Symptoms with a prevalence of 5% or greater were cough (17.8%), fatigue (16.8%), sleep disturbance (9.3%), and diarrhea (5.6%) at the 1‐month follow‐up; cough (15.3%), fatigue (13.3%), sleep disturbance (9.2%), and poor appetite (5.1%) at the 3‐month follow‐up; and cough (12.0%), fatigue (7.6%), sleep disturbance (5.4%), and poor concentration (5.4%) at the 6‐month follow‐up (Table 2). Cough, fatigue, and sleep disturbance were most common at all follow‐up points (Figure 2). ANCOVA for these frequent symptoms revealed that sleep disturbance was significantly associated with change in QOL score from admission at all three follow‐ups (mean difference 9.25, 20.15, and 19.81; 95% CI, 1.58–16.91, 3.38–36.92, and 5.51–34.11), any symptoms at 3‐month follow up (mean difference 8.77; 95% CI, 1.21–16.33), and cough at 6‐month follow‐up (mean difference 19.31; 95% CI, 0.45–38.17) (Table 4).

caption.p: The prevalence of each long‐COVID symptom. Respiratory symptoms included cough and rhinorrhea. ME/CFS symptoms included fatigue, postexertional malaise, sleep disturbance, myalgia, arthralgia, and headache. Others included anxiety, breathing difficulty, decreased motivation, feelings of feverishness, hearing loss, hoarseness, irritability, palpitations, swallowing difficulty, and tinnitus.
Associations between frequent symptoms of long‐COVID and quality of life score among children.

tr.th: Change in quality of life score from admission

fn.p: Note: The quality of life (QOL) score of children was assessed through KINDL questionnaire answered by parents; the Kiddy‐KINDL® questionnaire was used for children aged 3–6 years asnd KINDL® questionnaire for children aged 7–17 years. The total score calculated ranged from 0 to 100, with higher scores indicating better QOL. To compare change in QOL score, analysis of covariance was performed, adjusted for age and sex of children.

sec.p: Figure 3 shows the prevalence of any symptoms, respiratory symptoms, and ME/CFS symptoms by age categories at all three follow‐ups. Although the overall prevalence of long‐COVID symptoms decreased over time, ME/CFS symptoms remained present in 5.9% of children aged 0–6 years, 25.0% of children aged 7–17 years, and 34.4% of parents at the 6‐month follow‐up. Fisher's exact test revealed that the prevalence of ME/CSF symptoms among ages 0–6 years was significantly lower than ages 7–17 years at the 1‐ and 3‐month follow‐ups (p < 0.05 and p < 0.01), and parents at all three follow‐ups (p < 0.001, p < 0.001, and p < 0.001). There was no significant difference in the prevalence of ME/CFS symptoms between ages 7 and 17 years and parents.

caption.p: The prevalence of long‐COVID symptoms by age categories at 1‐, 3‐, and 6‐month follow‐ups. Respiratory symptoms included cough and rhinorrhea. ME/CFS symptoms included fatigue, postexertional malaise, sleep disturbance, myalgia, arthralgia, and headache. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

sec.p: Abdominal pain, chest pain, diarrhea, dizziness, hair loss, headache, numbness, olfactory/gustatory disorders, rhinorrhea, and sore throat were rarely reported in children at the 6‐month follow‐up (0%–1%). Of these, none of the children reported olfactory/gustatory disorders during all the follow‐up periods of this study (some parents had olfactory/gustatory disorders) (Figure 2). Fisher's exact test for each symptom revealed that the prevalence of fatigue was significantly higher among parents than children at all three follow‐ups (Table 2).

The most frequent symptoms among parents affected by COVID‐19 were fatigue (42.1%), cough (23.7%), and sleep disturbance (19.7%) at the 1‐month follow‐up; fatigue (32.8%), sleep disturbance (18.0%), and impairment of concentration (11.5%) at the 3‐month follow‐up; and fatigue (31.3%), sleep disturbance (12.5%), impairment of concentration (7.8%), and cough (7.8%) at the 6‐month follow‐up, and 40.6% of parents still had any symptoms at 6 months, higher than in children (Figure 2).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: In this study, we have shown that 23% of children with COVID‐19 had long‐COVID symptoms at 6 months after the SARS‐CoV‐2 infection. There were no specific risk factors for long‐COVID. Among the long‐COVID symptoms, cough, fatigue, and sleep disturbance were highly prevalent at 1‐, 3‐, and 6‐month follow‐ups, with sleep disturbance associated with lower QOL score at all follow‐up points. Children aged 7–17 years and parents were more likely to develop ME/CFS symptoms compared to children aged 0–6 years. To our knowledge, this may be the first prospective cohort study to compare long‐COVID symptoms between children and parents.

The prevalence of long‐COVID symptoms among children in previous studies was highly variable. A few systematic reviews reported that 25.2% of pediatric patients had sequelae of COVID‐19 more than 1 month after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection 13 and 16.2% after at least 3 months. 19 However, age groups, the definitions of sequelae of COVID‐19, and data collection rates in the included studies were very heterogeneous. In addition, whereas the prevalence of long‐COVID is highly dependent on the time elapsed since onset, most studies were cross‐sectional, with different timing of surveys, and consequently, prevalence rates were variable. Our study was a prospective longitudinal study with a high data collection rate and showed the prevalence of long‐COVID was 44.9%, 37.8%, and 22.8% at 1, 3, and 6 months after SARS‐CoV‐2 infection, which decreased gradually over time, but was higher overall than in past reports. In a registry study conducted by the Japan Pediatric Society between February 2020 and April 2022, 1697 of a total of 5411 children (<16 years) with COVID‐19 were analyzed for clinical symptoms after the acute phase, of which 55 (3.2%) had symptoms 28 days after the onset of COVID‐19. 20 In contrast, our study found that 22.8% of patients experienced symptoms at 6‐month follow‐up. Possible reasons for this discrepancy could be that approximately 40% of the participants in the registry study were outpatient cases. The main symptoms of long‐COVID identified in the registry study were olfactory/gustatory disorder (1.1%/1.0%), fever (0.8%), fatigue (0.7%), and cough (0.5%). Although fatigue and cough were also prevalent in our study, olfactory/gustatory disorder and fever were rarely reported in our findings. This difference may be attributed to the differences in the SARS‐CoV‐2 variants over the study periods.

Previous studies suggested that increasing age, female patients, obesity, severe acute COVID‐19, and special healthcare needs are risk factors for long‐COVID in children as well as in adults. 4 , 9 , 10 , 13 , 21 , 22 , 23 In this study, there was no significant difference in the prevalence of long‐COVID among children with age, sex, severity of acute COVID‐19, and vaccination against SARS‐CoV‐2. However, increasing age was a risk factor in the analysis limited to ME/CFS symptoms. Previous studies that suggested increasing age is a risk for long‐COVID mainly incorporated adults and adolescents who were prone to ME/CFS symptoms. 6 , 8 Increasing age was not a risk factor for overall long‐COVID symptoms in this study, probably because the study included a relatively large number of younger children. Vaccination against SARS‐CoV‐2 has been shown to be effective in preventing long‐COVID in adults, 24 but there were few studies that have examined the effects of vaccines on long‐COVID in children. In this study, vaccination did not affect the prevalence of long‐COVID among children. Because of the small number of vaccinated participants in our cohort, further studies are desired to clarify the association of vaccination with long‐COVID. Patients with obesity or special healthcare needs were not included in the study; nevertheless, the high prevalence of long‐COVID among our participants is alarming. It should be also noted that long‐COVID can occur not infrequently, even if pediatric patients are young, not obese, nonsevere COVID‐19, or have no special healthcare needs.

Cough is a common symptom in long‐COVID in adults but is noted less frequently in children. 5 , 13 By contrast, cough was relatively common in younger children in this study. This may be because we investigated symptoms at each follow‐up, which increased the cough frequency in younger children, who had more opportunities to contract respiratory infections. However, as many previous studies did not include younger children to a large extent, cough as a symptom of long‐COVID in children might have been underestimated. Further studies with a control group are needed to clarify whether cough as a long‐COVID symptom is more likely to occur in younger children.

Fatigue and sleep disturbance are among the primary symptoms of ME/CFS, which is a complex syndrome characterized by fatigue and a substantial loss of physical and cognitive function. 25 Other symptoms (poor concentration, dizziness, headache, and arthralgia/myalgia) commonly seen in ME/CFS were also identified in our study and reported to occur after COVID‐19 infection in both adults and children. 5 , 7 , 9 , 21 , 23 , 26 , 27 , 28 Children with ME/CFS may have frequent school absences, become dependent on a wheelchair, or become housebound or bedridden; similar situations have been reported in children after COVID‐19 infection. 5 , 27 , 29 , 30 Prevalence of ME/CFS is high among children 10–19 years of age, 31 whereas a small number of patients under 5 years of age have also been reported. 32 This age trend was similar to our results with fewer patients presenting with ME/CFS symptoms in the 0–6 years age group, but higher in the 7–17 years age group. It is unclear whether ME/CFS symptoms are direct results of the SARS‐CoV‐2 infection or due to immune‐mediated processes, such as cytokines. These symptoms have been reported in patients with severe acute respiratory syndrome and Middle East respiratory syndrome. 6 , 33 , 34 Although adolescents are prone to dysautonomia triggered by nonspecific infections, 35 the susceptibility to ME/CFS symptoms may be a characteristic of coronaviruses.

Children have tended to be overlooked during this pandemic because the clinical severity of COVID‐19 in the acute phase is milder than in adults, 36 , 37 and some studies suggested that fewer children suffered from long‐COVID. 10 , 26 , 38 However, it has recently become clear that some children struggle to return to normal life because of long‐COVID. 27 , 29 , 39 This prospective study showed that children experience long‐COVID more often than previously expected. Our study has also revealed that sleep disturbance in long‐COVID was associated with low QOL score up to 6 months after the onset of COVID‐19. As there is currently no standard treatment plan for them, further research leading to a better comprehension of long‐COVID is desirable.

In a follow‐up study of 1066 adult patients in Japan with a history of hospitalization for COVID‐19 between January 2020 and February 2021, the following symptoms at 6‐month follow‐up were similar to our study: fatigue (16.0%), impairment of concentration (11.2%), and sleep disturbances (9.7%) were common. 40 The percentage of patients with any symptoms was high at 40.5% at 6‐month follow‐up, consistent with the 40.6% in our study. By contrast, olfactory and gustatory disorders at 7.9% and 6.7%, respectively, were not reported in our study, which may be due to differences in the SARS‐CoV‐2 variants in different study periods.

This study has several limitations. First, it was a single‐center study with all Japanese participants. Differences in long‐COVID by nationality and ethnicity have scarcely been investigated; thus, the results of this study might not be generalizable to other populations. However, to the best of our knowledge, this may be the first cohort study of long‐COVID among Japanese children, contributing to the accumulation of data on diverse populations. Second, we did not examine the variants of SARS‐CoV‐2 in each participant. Although the omicron variants of concern were predominant in Japan during this study period, it was impossible to strictly exclude the effect of different variants on severity and symptoms. Third, regarding the relationship between the severity of acute COVID‐19 and the prevalence of long‐COVID, although it would be more beneficial to examine the severity of each clinical symptom in the acute phase, there are no established classification criteria for the severity of each clinical symptom, and the number of cases in this study was insufficient to perform statistical analysis on the relationship between the severity of each symptom. Therefore, it was difficult to examine the association between the severity of each symptom and the prevalence of long‐COVID in this study. Finally, we collected symptoms present at each follow‐up rather than those persisting from the acute phase of COVID‐19. Thus, we could not distinguish between long‐COVID symptoms and those caused by other factors, such as viral infections, potentially leading to overestimating some symptom prevalence rates. Further studies with a control group will help to more clearly differentiate symptoms specific to long‐COVID from those caused by other factors. In contrast, the strength of this study is that it is longitudinal with a high follow‐up rate and is relatively free from recall bias. As the time elapsed from the onset of COVID‐19 affects long‐COVID prevalence, our findings obtained at a certain evaluation timing should contribute to the healthcare practice of primary care physicians treating long‐COVID. Additionally, a notable strength of this study is the inclusion of long‐COVID data from both children and their corresponding parents.

In conclusion, 22.8% of children with COVID‐19 had long‐COVID at 6 months after onset regardless of age, sex, or severity of the acute phase. Some long‐COVID symptoms resemble ME/CFS symptoms and may seriously interfere with the children's lives.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: All authors contributed to the study conception and design. HI performed data collection and analysis. HI wrote the first draft of the manuscript. TF and MK modified and reviewed the manuscript. MK supervised and revised the manuscript. All authors approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: All authors do not have any potential, perceived, or real conflicts of interest.

caption.p: Table S1. List of clinical symptoms investigated during hospitalization and in the questionnaire at each follow‐up.
Table S2. Clinical characteristics and demographic data for children in long‐COVID and nonlong‐COVID groups at the 6‐month follow‐up.
Table S3. Multivariable logistic regression to examine risk factors for ME/CFS symptoms of long‐COVID.
Table S4. Incidence of long‐COVID symptoms among children (0–6, 7–17 years) and parents.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
ack.p: This study was supported by JSPS KAKENHI (Grant Number JP22K02483). The authors wish to thank Takanobu Maekawa, Yoko Takahashi, Kei Sakamoto, Yo Murata, and Takuma Yamai for their assistance in creating the questionnaire and research design.
